key_insight: Elastic response of HCP titanium is size- and strain-rate independent, but plastic deformation becomes strongly size dependent, with larger models producing more stable and homogeneous behavior.
engineering_relevance: For atomistic compression studies of titanium, model size must be matched to strain rate to obtain reliable plasticity predictions and avoid misleading fluctuations in the simulated response.
method: Molecular-dynamics simulations of crystalline alpha-titanium under uniaxial compression
System size varied across four orders of magnitude, up to 32 million atoms
Strain rate studied down to 10^8 s^-1
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that elastic properties were unchanged by system size or strain rate, while larger systems reduced stress fluctuations and stabilized dislocation activity during plastic deformation.
watch_item: Very small cells or overly fast loading rates can under-resolve plastic mechanisms, so size–rate coupling must be checked before trusting deformation results.
paper_title: Uniaxial compression of crystalline HCP titanium: an atomistic modelling study of size effects
